answer:
In *Andreas*, the apostle Andrew confronts the cannibalistic Mermedonians—a brutal, "corrupt folk" who devour strangers—through heroic endurance of torture and displays of divine power that punish the wicked and reward repentance.[^1] Andrew is imprisoned, scourged repeatedly over days, and challenged by devils urging the pagans to kill him, yet he remains steadfast, praising God.[^2] God intervenes directly: Andrew commands a stone pillar to unleash a flood that drowns the worst offenders, resurrects drowned youths, and drives off demons, leading the survivors to mass conversion, baptism, and the building of a church.[^3] The strategy is coercive and miraculous, emphasizing physical suffering as proof of faith, supernatural retribution to break pagan resistance, and collective salvation for the humbled populace.

In *Elene*, Helena confronts the Jewish elders of Jerusalem—not violent cannibals, but scriptural scholars who reject Christ despite prophecies—via inquisitorial debate and coercion to reveal the True Cross.[^4] She summons thousands of "law-clever" Jews in stages (3,000, then 1,000, then 500), citing Moses, David, and Isaiah to expose their denial of Christ, threatening mass execution by fire if they lie.[^5] They yield Judas, who resists until starved in a pit for seven days, then prays for a sign; smoke reveals the Cross's location, a resurrection miracle identifies the True Cross, and a devil's outburst is rebutted by Judas (now Spirit-filled), prompting his baptism, installation as bishop Cyriacus, and broader Jewish conversion through the relic's veneration.[^6] The strategy is rhetorical and relic-centered: scriptural argumentation, targeted coercion of an intellectual elite, and evidential miracle (resurrection via Cross) to establish institutional Christianity.

The evangelical approaches thus differ sharply: *Andreas* relies on the saint's passive martyrdom and aggressive divine judgments to shatter a savage, unlettered paganism through awe and fear; *Elene* deploys active disputation, threats, and relic-discovery to overcome a learned but obstinate Judaism via proof from their own traditions and tangible holy objects.

[^1]: *Andreas*, [Chapter 1/translation/#p-da0c5e](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-da0c5e).
[^2]: *Andreas*, [Chapter 1/translation/#p-5e2611](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-5e2611); [Chapter 1/translation/#p-51b3f4](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-51b3f4).
[^3]: *Andreas*, [Chapter 1/translation/#p-2e50c1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-2e50c1); [Chapter 1/translation/#p-0609a7](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-0609a7).
[^4]: *Elene*, [Chapter 1/translation/#p-7b1c1f](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-7b1c1f).
[^5]: *Elene*, [Chapter 1/translation/#p-f5d1f1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).
[^6]: *Elene*, [Chapter 1/translation/#p-f5d1f1](https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-elene-d2d132/01/translation/#p-f5d1f1).
